lessons.title: Stage 1 · Basic Vowels
lessons.subtitle: Learn all 10 Korean vowels by shape and sound
lessons.learningObjective: Recognize and pronounce all 10 basic Korean vowels, grouping them by shape family.

vocabulary.memoryHook: A vertical line with one right arm — mouth opens wide to say 'ah'
Two right arms — double the arms, add a Y: 'yah'
One left arm — mirror of ㅏ, like saying 'uh' with surprise
Two left arms — double the arms, add a Y: 'yuh'
Short line reaching UP — lips round up to say 'oh'
Two lines reaching up — double up, add Y: 'yo'
Short line hanging DOWN — lips push down to say 'oo'
Two lines hanging down — double down, add Y: 'yoo'
A single flat line — flat mouth, flat sound: 'eu'
A single vertical line — smile shape, say 'ee'

pronunciationTips.description: These two vowels confuse many beginners because both involve rounded lips.
pronunciationTips.englishComparison: ㅓ (eo) is closer to the 'u' in English 'sun' or 'uh', while ㅗ (o) is a clean round 'oh' like in 'go'.
pronunciationTips.commonMistake: Do not pronounce ㅓ as 'oh'. Keep your mouth more open and relaxed for ㅓ.

pronunciationTips.description: Both are simple shapes but produce very different sounds.
pronunciationTips.englishComparison: ㅡ (eu) has no direct English equivalent — keep your lips flat and unsmiling. ㅣ (i) is 'ee' as in 'see'.
pronunciationTips.commonMistake: Do not smile when saying ㅡ. Keep your jaw slightly open and lips neutral.

memoryHooks.story: Korean vowels split into two families: vertical vowels (ㅏ ㅑ ㅓ ㅕ ㅣ) grow arms left or right from a vertical stem, while horizontal vowels (ㅗ ㅛ ㅜ ㅠ ㅡ) grow branches up or down from a horizontal beam.
memoryHooks.visualDescription: Imagine a tree trunk standing tall for vertical vowels, and a bridge lying flat for horizontal vowels.

memoryHooks.story: Any vowel with two branches instead of one adds a Y sound at the start. One arm = plain, two arms = Y-version.
memoryHooks.visualDescription: Single branch: ㅏ ㅓ ㅗ ㅜ. Double branch: ㅑ ㅕ ㅛ ㅠ.

scriptItems.text: Korean has exactly 10 basic vowels. They split neatly into shape families that tell you how to move your mouth.
scriptItems.translation: Learn the shape pattern and the sounds will follow.

speech.speechText: 한글에는 기본 모음이 열 개 있어요.

scriptItems.text: Vertical vowels: ㅏ (a), ㅑ (ya), ㅓ (eo), ㅕ (yeo), ㅣ (i). These have a tall vertical stroke with arms pointing left or right.
scriptItems.romanization: vertical vowels a ya eo yeo i
scriptItems.translation: The direction of the arm changes the sound.

speech.speechText: 세로 모음: 아, 야, 어, 여, 이.

scriptItems.text: Horizontal vowels: ㅗ (o), ㅛ (yo), ㅜ (u), ㅠ (yu), ㅡ (eu). These have a flat horizontal stroke with branches above or below.
scriptItems.romanization: horizontal vowels o yo u yu eu
scriptItems.translation: Up means brighter, down means deeper.

speech.speechText: 가로 모음: 오, 요, 우, 유, 으.

writingTargets.prompt: Trace ㅏ: draw a vertical line, then one short horizontal stroke to the right.
Trace ㅑ: vertical line with two short strokes to the right.
Trace ㅓ: vertical line with one short stroke to the left.
Trace ㅕ: vertical line with two short strokes to the left.
Trace ㅗ: horizontal line with one short stroke going up.
Trace ㅛ: horizontal line with two short strokes going up.
Trace ㅜ: horizontal line with one short stroke going down.
Trace ㅠ: horizontal line with two short strokes going down.
Trace ㅡ: a single flat horizontal line.
Trace ㅣ: a single vertical line.

readingDrills.prompt: Which is the Y-version? Read it

dialogueItems.title: Vertical vowel pairs

lines.speaker: Coach
lines.translation: Listen: one arm right, one arm left.

lines.speaker: You
lines.translation: I can see the mirror pattern.

dialogueItems.comprehensionQuestion: Which vowel has the arm pointing left?

dialogueItems.title: Horizontal vowel pairs

lines.speaker: Coach
lines.translation: Listen: one branch up, one branch down.

lines.speaker: You
lines.translation: Up is oh, down is oo.

dialogueItems.comprehensionQuestion: Which vowel has the branch pointing upward?

checkpointItems.prompt: Which symbol is the vertical vowel with one right arm?
checkpointItems.explanation: ㅏ has one horizontal stroke pointing right from a vertical stem, producing the 'a' sound.

checkpointItems.prompt: How do you add a Y sound to a basic vowel?
checkpointItems.explanation: One branch = plain vowel, two branches = Y-version. For example ㅏ (a) becomes ㅑ (ya).

checkpointItems.prompt: Which vowel makes the 'eu' sound with a flat horizontal shape?
checkpointItems.explanation: ㅡ is a single flat horizontal line. Keep your lips neutral and slightly open.

lessons.title: Stage 2 · Basic Consonants
lessons.subtitle: Meet all 14 consonants that anchor Korean syllables
lessons.learningObjective: Recognize all 14 basic Korean consonants, connect their shapes to mouth movements, and learn their Korean names.

vocabulary.memoryHook: Looks like the number 7 or a gun — 'g' sound
An L-shape or a nose bridge — 'n' sound
A table or door frame — 'd' sound
A winding river or tongue rolling — 'r/l' sound
A square box like closed lips — 'm' sound
A bucket shape — 'b' sound
A mountain peak or sail — 's' sound
A zero or circle — silent at start, 'ng' at end
A hat or zigzag — 'j' sound
ㅈ with extra stroke — aspirated 'ch' sound
ㄱ with extra stroke — aspirated 'k' sound
ㄷ with extra stroke — aspirated 't' sound
ㅂ with extra stroke — aspirated 'p' sound
A person wearing a hat — 'h' sound with breath

pronunciationTips.description: Korean ㄱ is softer than English hard 'g' or 'k'. It sits between the two.
pronunciationTips.englishComparison: Think of a gentle 'g' at the start of a word, closer to the 'g' in 'ago' than the 'k' in 'kite'.
pronunciationTips.commonMistake: Do not blast too much air. Save the strong puff for aspirated ㅋ.

pronunciationTips.description: ㄹ sounds like a light 'r' between vowels and more like an 'l' at the end of a syllable.
pronunciationTips.englishComparison: It is a flap, like the quick 'd' in American English 'butter'. Not a rolling R and not a heavy L.
pronunciationTips.commonMistake: Do not use a strong English R or L. Keep the tongue tap light and quick.

pronunciationTips.description: ㅇ is silent when it appears at the start of a syllable block, but becomes 'ng' when it appears as the final consonant (batchim).
pronunciationTips.englishComparison: At the start: zero sound, just a placeholder. At the end: 'ng' as in 'sing'.
pronunciationTips.commonMistake: Do not try to pronounce ㅇ at the beginning of a syllable — it is only there because every Korean block needs a consonant slot.

memoryHooks.story: Many Hangul consonants are tiny blueprints of what your mouth, tongue, or throat does. ㄱ is the tongue back shape, ㄴ is the tongue tip touching the roof, ㅁ is lips closed, ㅅ is teeth, ㅇ is throat.
memoryHooks.visualDescription: Each shape hints at the body part that makes the sound.

memoryHooks.story: The aspirated consonants (ㅊ ㅋ ㅌ ㅍ) are just the basic consonants (ㅈ ㄱ ㄷ ㅂ) with an extra stroke. That extra stroke means extra air.
memoryHooks.visualDescription: Basic shape + extra line = more breath.

scriptItems.text: Korean has 14 basic consonants. Every syllable block starts with a consonant — even pure vowel sounds use the silent placeholder ㅇ.
scriptItems.translation: The consonant always comes first in a block.

speech.speechText: 한글에는 기본 자음이 열네 개 있어요.

scriptItems.text: Consonants come in families: plain (ㄱ ㄷ ㅂ ㅈ), aspirated (ㅋ ㅌ ㅍ ㅊ), and unique shapes (ㄴ ㄹ ㅁ ㅅ ㅇ ㅎ).
scriptItems.translation: Learning the family structure helps you remember all fourteen.

scriptItems.text: Aspirated consonants add a burst of air. Hold your hand in front of your mouth: ㅋ ㅌ ㅍ ㅊ should make you feel a strong puff.
scriptItems.romanization: aspirated consonants add a burst of air
scriptItems.translation: Feel the difference with your hand.

speech.speechText: 격음은 숨이 많이 나와요. 손을 입 앞에 대 보세요.

writingTargets.prompt: Trace ㄱ: start at top, go right, then straight down.
Trace ㄴ: go down, then right along the bottom.
Trace ㄷ: a horizontal top, then down-right like a table.
Trace ㄹ: a winding zigzag shape.
Trace ㅁ: a simple closed square.
Trace ㅂ: like a bucket with two sides and a bottom.
Trace ㅅ: two lines meeting at a peak.
Trace ㅇ: a simple circle.
Trace ㅈ: like ㅅ with a flat top.
Trace ㅊ: like ㅈ with an extra horizontal stroke on top.
Trace ㅋ: like ㄱ with an extra horizontal stroke.
Trace ㅌ: like ㄷ with an extra horizontal stroke.
Trace ㅍ: like ㅂ with an extra horizontal stroke through the top.
Trace ㅎ: a hat shape with a circle beneath.

readingDrills.prompt: Which is the aspirated version of ㄱ?

dialogueItems.title: Name the letter
dialogueItems.comprehensionQuestion: What was the answer letter name?

dialogueItems.title: Plain vs aspirated

lines.speaker: Coach
lines.translation: How are ㄷ and ㅌ different?

dialogueItems.comprehensionQuestion: Which consonant has more air: ㄷ or ㅌ?

checkpointItems.prompt: Which consonant looks like a closed square representing closed lips?
checkpointItems.explanation: ㅁ (mieum) looks like a box, representing the lips pressed together for 'm'.

checkpointItems.prompt: What does ㅇ do at the beginning of a syllable?
checkpointItems.explanation: ㅇ (ieung) is a placeholder at the start. It only makes 'ng' when used as a final consonant.

checkpointItems.prompt: Which consonant is the aspirated version of ㅂ?
checkpointItems.explanation: ㅍ (pieup) is ㅂ (bieup) with an extra stroke, adding more air to the sound.

checkpointItems.prompt: Which consonant sounds like 'r' between vowels and 'l' at the end?
checkpointItems.explanation: ㄹ (rieul) is a unique Korean sound that shifts between r and l depending on position.

lessons.title: Stage 3 · Build Syllables
lessons.subtitle: Combine consonants and vowels into readable blocks
lessons.learningObjective: Read and write CV (consonant + vowel) syllable blocks by combining the letters learned in Stages 1 and 2.

vocabulary.memoryHook: ㄱ plus ㅏ becomes 가 — the consonant leads, the vowel follows.
ㄴ plus ㅏ becomes 나 — the Korean word for 'I'.
ㄷ plus ㅏ becomes 다.
ㄹ plus ㅏ becomes 라.
ㅁ plus ㅏ becomes 마.
ㅂ plus ㅏ becomes 바.
ㅅ plus ㅏ becomes 사.
ㅇ plus ㅏ becomes 아 — the ㅇ is silent here.
ㅈ plus ㅏ becomes 자.
ㅎ plus ㅏ becomes 하.

pronunciationTips.description: Read the consonant and vowel as one smooth beat, not two separate sounds.
pronunciationTips.englishComparison: Think of 'ga' as one syllable like in English 'garage', not 'g' + 'ah' spoken separately.
pronunciationTips.commonMistake: Do not pause between the consonant and vowel. They form a single block and a single beat.

pronunciationTips.description: Vertical vowels (ㅏ ㅓ etc.) sit to the right of the consonant. Horizontal vowels (ㅗ ㅜ etc.) sit below the consonant.
pronunciationTips.englishComparison: The block layout changes based on vowel orientation, but the reading order stays the same: consonant first, then vowel.
pronunciationTips.commonMistake: Do not read horizontal-vowel blocks top-to-bottom as two syllables. 고 is one beat: 'go'.

memoryHooks.story: Korean is not written in a long chain of letters like English. Each syllable snaps into a compact block, like a tiny building made of Lego pieces.
memoryHooks.visualDescription: Consonant + vowel locked into one readable unit.

scriptItems.text: Now we stop naming letters and start reading blocks. Every Hangul block has at least one consonant and one vowel.
scriptItems.romanization: now we stop naming letters and start reading blocks
scriptItems.translation: This is where Hangul starts feeling useful.

speech.speechText: 이제는 글자 이름보다 글자 블록을 읽기 시작해요.

scriptItems.text: Vertical vowels sit beside the consonant: 가 나 다. Horizontal vowels sit below: 고 누 두.
scriptItems.romanization: vertical vowels sit beside, horizontal vowels sit below
scriptItems.translation: The vowel shape determines the block layout.

speech.speechText: 세로 모음은 옐에, 가로 모음은 아래에 위치해요.

writingTargets.prompt: Write 가: place ㄱ on the left, ㅏ on the right.
Write 나: place ㄴ on the left, ㅏ on the right.
Write 다: place ㄷ on the left, ㅏ on the right.
Write 마: place ㅁ on the left, ㅏ on the right.
Write 사: place ㅅ on the left, ㅏ on the right.

readingDrills.prompt: Read this block with a horizontal vowel

lines.speaker: Coach
lines.translation: Build this block. The vowel goes below.

checkpointItems.prompt: Which block is ㄴ + ㅏ?
checkpointItems.explanation: ㄴ (nieun) combined with ㅏ (a) forms 나.

checkpointItems.prompt: Which block is ㅁ + ㅗ?
checkpointItems.explanation: ㅁ on top with ㅗ below forms 모.

checkpointItems.prompt: Where does a horizontal vowel like ㅗ sit in the block?
options: To the right of the consonant
checkpointItems.explanation: Horizontal vowels (ㅗ, ㅜ, ㅡ, etc.) are placed below the consonant in the syllable block.

checkpointItems.prompt: Read this block: 하
checkpointItems.explanation: ㅎ (hieut) + ㅏ (a) = 하 (ha).

lessons.title: Stage 4 · Double Consonants
lessons.subtitle: Learn the 5 tense (ssang) consonants and review aspirated sounds
lessons.learningObjective: Recognize all 5 double (tense) consonants and distinguish them from their plain and aspirated counterparts.

vocabulary.memoryHook: Double ㄱ pressed tight — tense 'kk'
Double ㄷ stacked — tense 'tt'
Double ㅂ merged — tense 'pp'
Double ㅅ peaks — tense 'ss'
Double ㅈ hats — tense 'jj'

pronunciationTips.description: Korean has three levels for many consonants: plain (soft), tense (tight throat, no extra air), and aspirated (strong puff of air).
pronunciationTips.englishComparison: Plain ㄱ is between English g/k. Tense ㄲ is a tight, clipped 'kk' with no air. Aspirated ㅋ is like English 'k' in 'kite' with a big puff.
pronunciationTips.commonMistake: Do not confuse tense with loud. Tense means your throat muscles tighten, not that you shout.

pronunciationTips.description: To produce a tense consonant, tighten your throat slightly and cut off airflow. The sound should feel compressed and sharp.
pronunciationTips.englishComparison: Imagine saying 'uh-oh' — the catch in your throat before 'oh' is similar to the tension in tense consonants.
pronunciationTips.commonMistake: Do not release a burst of air. Tense sounds are the opposite of aspirated sounds.

memoryHooks.story: All five tense consonants are visually doubled versions of basic consonants. Double the shape, tighten the sound.

scriptItems.text: Korean has 5 double consonants called ssang (쌍). They look like two copies of a basic consonant merged together, and they produce a tense, sharp sound.
scriptItems.romanization: korean has 5 double consonants called ssang

scriptItems.text: Compare the three levels: 가 (plain ga) vs 까 (tense kka) vs 카 (aspirated ka). Each starts differently.
scriptItems.translation: Train your ear to hear three distinct beginnings.

speech.speechText: 가, 까, 카. 세 가지 시작 소리가 다 달라요.

writingTargets.prompt: Trace ㄲ: two ㄱ shapes pressed together.
Trace ㄸ: two ㄷ shapes stacked.
Trace ㅃ: two ㅂ shapes merged.
Trace ㅆ: two ㅅ peaks side by side.
Trace ㅉ: two ㅈ hats merged.

readingDrills.prompt: Read the plain sound, then the tense sound
Read the plain sound, then the tense sound
Read the plain sound, then the tense sound
Read the plain sound, then the tense sound

dialogueItems.title: Hear the three-way contrast

lines.speaker: You
lines.text: 세 가지 시작 소리가 다 달라요.
lines.romanization: se gaji sijak soriga da dallayo
lines.translation: I can hear three different starts.

speech.speechText: 세 가지 시작 소리가 다 달라요.

dialogueItems.comprehensionQuestion: Which sound had the tightest, sharpest start with no air puff?

checkpointItems.prompt: Which is the tense (double) version of ㅅ?
checkpointItems.explanation: ㅆ (ssang-siot) is two ㅅ shapes merged, producing a tense 'ss' sound.

checkpointItems.prompt: How many tense (double) consonants does Korean have?
checkpointItems.explanation: Korean has exactly 5 tense consonants: ㄲ ㄸ ㅃ ㅆ ㅉ.

checkpointItems.prompt: Which syllable uses the tense consonant ㄸ?
checkpointItems.explanation: ㄸ + ㅏ = 따 (tta). 다 uses plain ㄷ, 타 uses aspirated ㅌ.

lessons.title: Stage 5 · Compound Vowels
lessons.subtitle: Decode all 11 blended vowel combinations
lessons.learningObjective: Recognize and pronounce all 11 compound vowels by understanding which basic vowels combine to form them.

vocabulary.memoryHook: ㅏ + ㅣ merged = ae
ㅑ + ㅣ merged = yae
ㅓ + ㅣ merged = e
ㅕ + ㅣ merged = ye
ㅗ + ㅏ glide = wa
ㅗ + ㅐ glide = wae
ㅗ + ㅣ glide = oe (sounds like 'we')
ㅜ + ㅓ glide = wo
ㅜ + ㅔ glide = we
ㅜ + ㅣ glide = wi
ㅡ + ㅣ glide = ui (the tricky one)

pronunciationTips.description: Modern Korean speakers often pronounce ㅐ (ae) and ㅔ (e) almost identically. Do not worry if you cannot hear a difference.
pronunciationTips.englishComparison: Both are close to the 'e' in English 'bed'. Historically ㅐ was more open like 'cat'.
pronunciationTips.commonMistake: Do not stress about distinguishing them by ear. Focus on spelling — many native speakers rely on context too.

pronunciationTips.description: ㅢ is the trickiest compound vowel. Its pronunciation changes by position: at the start of a word it is 'ui', after a consonant it often sounds like 'i', and in the possessive particle 의 it sounds like 'e'.
pronunciationTips.englishComparison: No single English equivalent. Think of it as a quick 'eu' sliding into 'ee'.
pronunciationTips.commonMistake: Do not always pronounce it the same way. Listen to native speakers in different contexts.

memoryHooks.story: Every compound vowel is made from two basic vowels you already know. If you can spot the ingredients, you can read the blend.
memoryHooks.visualDescription: Like mixing paint colors: ㅗ (blue) + ㅏ (yellow) = ㅘ (green).

memoryHooks.story: Vowels starting with ㅗ or ㅜ create a 'w' glide: ㅘ (wa), ㅙ (wae), ㅚ (oe), ㅝ (wo), ㅞ (we), ㅟ (wi).
memoryHooks.visualDescription: The round vowel starts the glide, then slides into another vowel.

scriptItems.text: Compound vowels look busy, but they are just familiar pieces combined. Learn the recipe and the shape stops being scary.
scriptItems.romanization: compound vowels are familiar pieces combined
scriptItems.translation: Decode the ingredients, then read the result.

speech.speechText: 복합 모음은 복잡해 보여도 이미 배운 조각이 합쳐진 거예요.

scriptItems.text: Group 1: ㅏ/ㅓ-based merges with ㅣ produce ㅐ (ae), ㅒ (yae), ㅔ (e), ㅖ (ye). Group 2: w-glides from ㅗ or ㅜ produce ㅘ ㅙ ㅚ ㅝ ㅞ ㅟ. Plus the oddball ㅢ (ui).
scriptItems.translation: Knowing the groups makes 11 vowels manageable.

speech.speechText: 복합 모음은 두 그룹으로 나눌 수 있어요.

writingTargets.prompt: Trace ㅐ: ㅏ with an extra ㅣ stroke on the right.
Trace ㅔ: ㅓ with an extra ㅣ stroke on the right.
Trace ㅘ: ㅗ below and ㅏ to the right.
Trace ㅝ: ㅜ below and ㅓ to the right.
Trace ㅟ: ㅜ with ㅣ added to the right.
Trace ㅢ: ㅡ horizontal and ㅣ vertical together.

readingDrills.prompt: Read this real word with a compound vowel

dialogueItems.title: Blend the shape

lines.speaker: You
lines.translation: That blend is ㅘ, and 와 means 'wow'!

dialogueItems.comprehensionQuestion: What compound vowel is ㅗ + ㅏ?

dialogueItems.title: The ae/e confusion

lines.speaker: You
lines.translation: Right. I need to distinguish them by spelling.

dialogueItems.comprehensionQuestion: How do modern Korean speakers usually tell ㅐ and ㅔ apart?

checkpointItems.prompt: Which compound vowel is built from ㅗ + ㅏ?
checkpointItems.explanation: ㅘ (wa) combines the round ㅗ with ㅏ to create a w-glide.

checkpointItems.prompt: Which compound vowel is the trickiest, changing pronunciation by position?
checkpointItems.explanation: ㅢ (ui) can sound like 'ui', 'i', or 'e' depending on where it appears in a word.

checkpointItems.prompt: How many compound vowels does Korean have in total?
checkpointItems.explanation: Korean has 11 compound vowels: ㅐ ㅒ ㅔ ㅖ ㅘ ㅙ ㅚ ㅝ ㅞ ㅟ ㅢ.

lessons.title: Stage 6 · Batchim Basics
lessons.subtitle: Read syllables with final consonants at the bottom
lessons.learningObjective: Understand the 7 representative batchim (final consonant) sounds and read common beginner words that use them.

vocabulary.memoryHook: The ㅂ sound closes at the bottom as a final p-like sound. 밥 is one of the first words every learner needs.
ㄹ sits at the bottom and gives a soft 'l' ending.
ㄴ at the bottom gives a gentle 'n' ending.
ㄱ at the bottom gives a clipped 'k' ending.
ㅇ at the bottom finally makes its 'ng' sound.
ㄴ batchim again — same word means both 'eye' and 'snow'.
ㅁ at the bottom closes the lips for a soft 'm' ending.
ㅅ as batchim sounds like 't' (not 's'). Many different consonants neutralize to a 't' sound in final position.

pronunciationTips.description: A final consonant changes how the syllable ends. In Korean, final consonants are often unreleased — you position your mouth but do not fully push the sound out.
pronunciationTips.englishComparison: Like the 'p' in 'stop' when you cut the word short without a puff of air.
pronunciationTips.commonMistake: Do not add an extra vowel after the final consonant. '밥' is 'bap', not 'ba-peu'.

pronunciationTips.description: Although many different consonants can appear as batchim, they all reduce to just 7 sounds: ㄱ(k), ㄴ(n), ㄷ(t), ㄹ(l), ㅁ(m), ㅂ(p), ㅇ(ng).
pronunciationTips.englishComparison: For example, ㅅ ㅈ ㅊ ㅌ ㅎ all sound like 't' in final position.
pronunciationTips.commonMistake: Do not try to pronounce every batchim letter differently. Learn the 7 representative sounds first.

memoryHooks.story: Batchim means 'support' or 'floor'. The final consonant sits under the block like a support beam holding the syllable up.
memoryHooks.visualDescription: A floor beam under a syllable block.

memoryHooks.story: No matter how many different letters appear at the bottom, they all collapse into just 7 exit sounds. Think of it as 7 doors — every batchim exits through one of them.
memoryHooks.visualDescription: 7 doors labeled k, n, t, l, m, p, ng.

scriptItems.text: Once you see the final consonant at the bottom of a block, many beginner words suddenly become readable. This is a major confidence jump.
scriptItems.romanization: once you see the final consonant many words become readable

speech.speechText: 받침이 보이기 시작하면 초급 단어가 훨씬 잘 읽혀요.

scriptItems.text: Remember: all batchim sounds reduce to 7 representatives. ㄱ-type (k), ㄴ (n), ㄷ-type (t), ㄹ (l), ㅁ (m), ㅂ-type (p), ㅇ (ng).
scriptItems.translation: Seven sounds cover every possible ending.

writingTargets.prompt: Write 밥: ㅂ on left, ㅏ on right, ㅂ on bottom.
Write 물: ㅁ on top, ㅜ below it, ㄹ on the bottom.
Write 국: ㄱ on top, ㅜ below it, ㄱ on the bottom.

readingDrills.prompt: Read this word and identify the batchim
Read this word and identify the batchim
Read this word and identify the batchim
Read this word — notice ㅇ finally makes a sound!
Read this word — ㅅ as batchim sounds like 't'

dialogueItems.title: Ordering water
dialogueItems.comprehensionQuestion: Which word in the dialogue has a ㄹ batchim?

dialogueItems.title: Talking about food
dialogueItems.comprehensionQuestion: Which two words have batchim in this dialogue?

checkpointItems.prompt: Which word below has a final consonant (batchim)?
checkpointItems.explanation: 밥 has ㅂ as its batchim (final consonant), giving a clipped 'p' ending.

checkpointItems.prompt: What sound does ㅅ make when it appears as batchim (final position)?
checkpointItems.explanation: In final position, ㅅ neutralizes to a 't' sound. That is why 옷 is pronounced 'ot', not 'os'.

checkpointItems.prompt: How many representative batchim sounds are there in Korean?
checkpointItems.explanation: All final consonants reduce to 7 representative sounds: k, n, t, l, m, p, ng.
